title: Real-World Reinforcement Learning: Games, Robots, and More
concept: Reinforcement learning shows up far beyond games — in robotic arms, self-driving cars, and systems that decide how to allocate limited resources.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is exploitation in reinforcement learning?

options.text: Sticking with the action already known to work well
Always trying something brand new
Ignoring rewards completely

exercises.explain: Right! Exploitation means leaning on the action already known to pay off.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An agent that only ever explores, and never exploits what it's learned, will reliably earn the most total reward.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Constant exploring never settles on the good actions it already found — an agent needs a balance of both.

cards.title: Not Just for Games Anymore
cards.body: Reinforcement learning got famous by mastering board games and video games. But the same trial-and-reward idea now runs in warehouses, on roads, and inside power grids — anywhere something has to make a long sequence of decisions and learn from the outcome.

cards.title: The same idea, many different worlds
cards.body: Any place with an agent making actions inside an environment, guided by rewards, is a candidate for reinforcement learning — whether that environment is a chessboard, a city street, or a factory floor.

cards.title: A robotic arm learning to grip
cards.body: A warehouse robotic arm doesn't come pre-programmed with the perfect grip for every oddly-shaped box. Instead, it tries different grip angles and pressures, earning a reward each time it lifts a box without dropping it — gradually refining its grip through trial and error, the same way a person learns to hold a slippery object without crushing it.

cards.title: Three real environments, one common idea
cards.body: A game-playing agent earns points for winning matches. A self-driving car earns reward for a smooth, safe trip and penalties for hard braking or unsafe distances. A power-grid system earns reward for routing electricity efficiently and penalties for outages or waste — three completely different environments, same reward-driven learning loop.

cards.title: It's not only for flashy robots
cards.body: You might picture reinforcement learning only in humanoid robots or game-playing AI. In reality, plenty of unglamorous systems use it too — like software that decides how to allocate delivery trucks across a city, or how to split server capacity among competing tasks, always trying to maximize reward over time.

cards.title: Everyday agent, action, environment
cards.body: The same three roles, in two very different places:

left.text:
Agent: the arm
Action: grip angle & pressure

right.text:
Agent: the driving system
Action: steer, brake, accelerate

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of reinforcement learning being used outside of games?

options.text: A robotic arm learning the best grip through repeated attempts and rewards
A spreadsheet that adds up a column of numbers
A dictionary app that looks up word definitions

exercises.explain: Right! Learning a good grip through trial, reward, and penalty is reinforcement learning at work in a warehouse, not a game.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Reinforcement learning is only useful for game-playing agents, not for anything in the physical world.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! It also shows up in robotic arms, self-driving cars, and resource-allocation systems, among others.

exercises.sentence: A self-driving car agent earns a ___ for a smooth, safe trip and a penalty for unsafe driving.
exercises.explain: A reward is what reinforces the safe, smooth driving actions over many trips.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows reinforcement learning applied to resource allocation?

options.text: A system that learns over many days which delivery trucks to send to which neighborhoods to minimize wasted trips, earning reward for efficient routes
A calculator dividing 100 by 4
A photo app applying the same filter to every picture

exercises.explain: Right! Learning better truck assignments over time by earning reward for efficient routing is a resource-allocation use of reinforcement learning.

exercises.prompt: A power grid control system adjusts how electricity is routed between neighborhoods throughout the day, earning a reward for keeping the lights on efficiently and a penalty for outages. What are its "actions" in this scenario?

options.text: The specific routing choices it makes for electricity
The neighborhoods themselves
The outages it's trying to avoid
The reward it earns

exercises.explain: Exactly! The routing choices are the actions — the specific decisions the agent is allowed to make.

exercises.prompt: A hospital is testing a system that decides how to schedule nurses across shifts, earning a reward for balanced workloads and good patient coverage, and a penalty for understaffed shifts. Is this a valid use of reinforcement learning, and why?

options.text: Yes — there's an agent (the scheduler) taking actions (shift assignments) in an environment (the hospital), guided by rewards and penalties
No — reinforcement learning only applies to video games and robots
No — there's no agent making decisions in this scenario
Yes, but only because hospitals always use very advanced technology

exercises.explain: Exactly! Any setting with an agent choosing actions in an environment based on reward and penalty signals fits reinforcement learning — hospitals included.

exercises.prompt: A student says: "Reinforcement learning is basically just about building robots that can walk." What's the best correction to this claim?

options.text: Reinforcement learning is a general trial-and-reward approach that also applies to games, self-driving cars, and resource allocation — robots are just one example among many
The student is completely correct — walking robots are the only real use case
Reinforcement learning has nothing to do with robots at all
Reinforcement learning only works when there's no reward signal involved

exercises.explain: Exactly! Walking robots are one example, but the same agent-action-environment-reward loop shows up in games, driving, resource allocation, and far beyond. You've completed the Reinforcement Learning unit!
